1: "Praise the LORD! Let those who serve the LORD give praise — praise the name of the LORD!" — Psalm 113 opens the Egyptian Hallel (Pss 113–118), sung at Passover. Jesus and his disciples sang this at the Last Supper (Matthew 26:30: "when they had sung a hymn, they went out to the Mount of Olives"). The very psalms of the Hallel were on the lips of Christ as he moved toward Gethsemane and the cross. The praise of YHWH's servants in Psalms 113:1 was Christ's own praise on the night he was betrayed.
2: "Let the name of the LORD be praised from now and for all time to come!" — the eternal scope of praise. Philippians 2:9-11: "God exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name, that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow... and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord." The praise of YHWH's name "from now and for all time" is fulfilled in the eternal worship of Christ (Revelation 5:13: "To him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be praise and honor and glory and power, for ever and ever!").
3: "From the rising of the sun to where it sets, the name of the LORD deserves all praise!" — the geographical universality of praise, east to west. Malachi 1:11: "from the rising to the setting of the sun, my name will be great among the nations." Matthew 28:19: "Go and make disciples of all nations" — the universal spread of worship from east to west is the Great Commission in geographical terms. The name of Christ is now praised from the rising to the setting of the sun, fulfilling both Psalms 113:3 and Malachi 1:11.
4: "The LORD reigns above all nations; his glory soars above the very heavens" — the transcendent sovereignty of YHWH. Ephesians 1:20-21: Christ was raised and "seated at his right hand in the heavenly realms, far above all rule and authority, power and dominion, and every name that is invoked." The Lord who reigns above all nations in Psalms 113:4 is recognized in the exalted Christ — the one who has been placed above every power and who now exercises the universal sovereignty that the psalm celebrates.
5: "Who can compare with the LORD our God, who has his throne so high above" — the incomparability of YHWH, the mi khamokha tradition (Exodus 15:11). In Christ, the incomparability of YHWH becomes personal: "The Son is the radiance of God's glory and the exact representation of his being" (Hebrews 1:3). The one who asks "who can compare?" finds the answer in Christ — the only one who is truly the image of the incomparable God (Colossians 1:15).
6: "yet bends down to look at both heaven and earth?" — the paradox: the highest God condescends to look at what is below. The Incarnation is this condescension taken to its extreme: not merely bending to look but descending to dwell. Philippians 2:6-8: "Who, being in very nature God, did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage; rather, he made himself nothing, taking the very nature of a servant, being made in human likeness." The God who bends down to look in Psalms 113:6 is the God who bends down to become flesh in the Incarnation.
7: "He lifts the poor out of the dust and raises the needy from the garbage heap" — the divine reversal, lifting the lowest. Luke 1:52-53 (Mary's Magnificat): "He has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble. He has filled the hungry with good things but has sent the rich away empty." Mary's song of the Incarnation is a direct riff on Psalms 113:7-8 — the divine reversal that the psalm celebrates is the very reversal that the Incarnation sets in motion. The poor lifted from the dust is every soul whom Christ receives (Matthew 11:28-30; Luke 7:22).
8: "to seat them alongside princes — the princes of his own people" — the elevation to royal dignity. Revelation 1:6: Christ "has made us to be a kingdom and priests to serve his God and Father." The dusty-poor seated alongside princes in Psalms 113:8 are the community of the resurrection — the ones who will reign with Christ (Revelation 20:4; 2 Timothy 2:12: "if we endure, we will also reign with him"). The seating alongside princes is the eschatological dignity Christ bestows on those he raises from the ash heap.
9: "He gives the childless woman a home, turning her into a glad mother of children. Praise the LORD!" — the barren woman who becomes a fruitful mother: Sarah (Genesis 18:11-14), Rebekah (Genesis 25:21), Rachel (Genesis 30:22), Hannah (1 Sam 1-2), Elizabeth (Luke 1:7, 36). The last and greatest instance is Mary, the virgin who conceives by the Spirit (Luke 1:35). Christ is born of one for whom "the Mighty One has done great things" (Luke 1:49) — the child of a divine reversal of all natural expectation. The glad mother of children at the end of this Hallel-opening psalm is the type of Mary, and Mary is the type of the church which "is the mother of us all" (Galatians 4:26).
